hunts.theme: Mayors, Fires, and the Man Who Built Temple Street
hunts.narrativeArc: From a French mayor's infrastructure legacy, through the fire stations that once protected this hillside, to a bar that transforms Southern tradition with laboratory precision.

cluePool.clue: The neighborhood surrounding Temple Street here was officially designated in 2002 to honor a community that has lived in this area since the 1920s. Locals call it by its two-letter abbreviation: HiFi. What's the full name?
cluePool.historicalFact: Historic Filipinotown was designated by the LA City Council in 2002. Filipino immigrants settled here in the 1920s, making it one of the oldest Filipino communities in the US. The gateway arch at Temple and Beverly marks the neighborhood's entrance.

cluePool.clue: Temple Street is named after a man who sailed from Massachusetts to Los Angeles around 1828 and opened the city's first general store. He became a Mexican citizen and took a Spanish first name. What was his adopted first name?
cluePool.historicalFact: John Temple arrived in LA c. 1828, became 'Don Juan Temple,' married into the Cota family, and built the Temple Block — the commercial heart of early DTLA. His Rancho Los Cerritos became Long Beach.

cluePool.clue: Unscramble these letters: R-U-B-Y-A-D-E. They spell an avenue named after LA's 9th mayor — a French-Canadian who won his election by a single vote. Find where this avenue meets Temple Street.
cluePool.historicalFact: Prudent Beaudry transformed LA from a dusty pueblo into a functioning city with gas lighting, reservoirs, and 4,000 street trees. He served as the 9th mayor starting in 1874.

cluePool.clue: Two French-Canadian brothers shaped early Los Angeles. One became the 9th mayor in 1874 — he built reservoirs, laid gas lines, and planted 4,000 street trees. His brother served on the City Council. The mayor's avenue runs into a neighborhood named for the brother. What is the brother's first name?
cluePool.historicalFact: Victor Beaudry served on the LA City Council while his brother Prudent was mayor. Victor Heights — the hillside neighborhood above Temple Street — carries his name. The brothers arrived from Montreal in the 1850s.

cluePool.clue: The 101 and 110 freeways meet near here in a massive interchange that destroyed much of the original neighborhood fabric. But one memorial remains: a gateway arch marking HiFi's entrance stands at the intersection of Temple Street and which boulevard?
cluePool.historicalFact: The HiFi gateway arch at Temple and Beverly was installed after the 2002 designation. The 101/110 interchange, built in the 1950s-60s, demolished hundreds of homes and bisected the Filipino community.

cluePool.clue: I won my office by the slimmest margin possible. I planted trees by the thousands. I piped gas beneath dirt roads. I built reservoirs to hold water for a city that had none. My brother's name marks the hilltop; mine marks the avenue below. Who am I?
cluePool.historicalFact: Prudent Beaudry (1818-1893) won the 1874 mayoral election by a single vote. He was French-Canadian — one of the few non-Anglo mayors in 19th-century LA. He transformed the city from a dusty pueblo of 6,000 into a place with gas lighting, running water, and tree-lined streets.

cluePool.clue: In 1849, a downtown street was called Calle de los Chapules — Grasshopper Street. Legend said if grasshoppers crossed this road, the grape harvest was doomed. It became Pearl Street, then took a governor's name in 1897 — forcing the ORIGINAL street with that name to be renamed. What did the castoff street become?
cluePool.historicalFact: When Grasshopper St became Pearl St and then Figueroa in 1897, the original Figueroa a few blocks west had to be renamed Boylston. It's a street that lost its identity to a chain of name-bumping.

cluePool.clue: I was born in Montreal. I won my office by a single vote. I lit the streets with gas when they had only oil lamps. I built reservoirs when the city had no running water. What is my first name?
cluePool.historicalFact: Prudent Beaudry (1818-1893) was a French-Canadian immigrant who became LA's 9th mayor in 1874, winning by a single vote. He modernized the city with gas lighting, water infrastructure, and 4,000 street trees — transforming a dusty pueblo of 6,000 into a livable city. His brother Victor's name lives on in Victor Heights, the hillside above Beaudry Avenue.

cluePool.clue: A park near Temple Street in Historic Filipinotown shares its name with a Spanish word meaning 'unity.' Filipino War Veterans are memorialized nearby. What is the park called?
cluePool.historicalFact: Unidad Park serves as a community gathering space in Historic Filipinotown. The Filipino War Veterans Memorial nearby honors Filipino soldiers who fought alongside American forces in WWII, many of whom settled in this neighborhood after the war.

cluePool.clue: In 1849, this downtown avenue was called Charity Street. By 1886, property owners petitioned City Council for a name change — they were sick of jokes about 'living on charity.' The Council took pity. What avenue did Charity become?
cluePool.historicalFact: Grand Avenue was originally Charity Street on the 1849 Ord survey map. When residents petitioned for a change, they suggested Pacific Avenue, but that name was taken. The LA Daily Times reported Council 'took pity on the people who were sick of living on charity.'

cluePool.clue: Two of DTLA's most famous buildings sit on this avenue: a concert hall designed by Frank Gehry and a contemporary art museum with a honeycomb facade. The avenue itself was originally called Charity — until residents demanded something more dignified. What is it?
cluePool.historicalFact: Walt Disney Concert Hall (Gehry, 2003) and The Broad (Diller Scofidio + Renfro, 2015) both sit on Grand Avenue, the former Charity Street.

cluePool.clue: Find the 5-letter word hidden in this phrase: 'a flag ran down the hill.' That word is also the avenue that Charity Street became. Head there.
cluePool.historicalFact: flaGRANDown — Grand Avenue, renamed from Charity in 1886. The hidden word spans across 'flag,' 'ran,' and 'down.'

cluePool.clue: Five DTLA streets in order, going east from Figueroa: Flower, Hope, ___, Olive, Hill. One of these was originally called Charity. Fill in the blank.
cluePool.historicalFact: The 1849 Ord survey named these streets. Hope and Charity were neighbors — but while Hope kept its name, Charity was forced to change after 37 years of jokes.

cluePool.clue: Before the 1960s, Bunker Hill was a wealthy residential neighborhood of Victorian mansions. Urban renewal demolished almost everything. One woman born into slavery in 1818 had owned property on a street nearby — one of the first Black women to own land in LA. She won her freedom in an LA courtroom in 1856. Who was she?
cluePool.historicalFact: Biddy Mason (1818-1891) was born enslaved in Mississippi. She walked behind her enslaver's wagon from Mississippi to California. In 1856, she petitioned for freedom in LA and won. She bought property on Spring Street between 3rd and 4th, became a nurse, philanthropist, and one of the wealthiest Black women in early LA.

cluePool.clue: I drew the first American street map of Los Angeles in 1849. I roomed with William Tecumseh Sherman at West Point. I forced Robert E. Lee to surrender at Appomattox. The streets you're walking — I named them. Who am I?
cluePool.historicalFact: Lt. Edward O.C. Ord surveyed and mapped LA in 1849 for the U.S. Army. He named the streets — including Charity (now Grand), Hope, Spring, and Main. He later became a Civil War general and helped force Lee's surrender at Appomattox.

cluePool.clue: Pershing Square, the public park at 5th and Hill, has had four different names. It started as La Plaza Abaja, then 6th Street Park, then Central Park, before getting its current name in 1918 to honor a WWI general. What was the general's first name?
cluePool.historicalFact: General John J. 'Black Jack' Pershing commanded the American Expeditionary Forces in WWI. The park was renamed in his honor in 1918. Before that, as 'Central Park,' it was LA's attempt to rival New York's — at a fraction of the acreage.

The last street standing is the only one from Ord's 1849 survey that no one ever petitioned to rename. What is it?
cluePool.historicalFact: Grand (formerly Charity) is eliminated first. Hill goes next. Then Olive. Then Flower. Hope is the last one standing — and it's the only street from Ord's 1849 survey that kept both its original name and its dignity. No one petitioned to change it.

cluePool.clue: In 1935, a cafeteria opened on South Broadway with a radical Depression-era policy: diners could pay whatever they could afford — or nothing at all. The owner estimated that 10,000 people ate free during the first year. The restaurant survived for 80 years. What was it called?
cluePool.historicalFact: Clifton's Cafeteria at 648 S Broadway opened in 1935 with a 'pay what you wish' policy. Founder Clifford Clinton lost money for years but believed feeding people mattered more than profit. The restaurant's forest-themed interior — complete with a three-story redwood tree — became a DTLA landmark.
